name: iot-sensor-ingestion-platform-design
question: A connected-devices platform ingests sensor readings from 50,000 field devices, each pushing a reading every 10 seconds. The current system accepts every reading over HTTP and writes it straight to a relational database; the same database also powers the live operations dashboard and historical trend reports. During peak hours, write latency exceeds 5 seconds, the dashboard goes stale, and analytical queries lock the same tables that ingestion needs. Redesign the platform so ingestion stays fast under sustained load, real-time and historical query paths no longer contend for the same resources, and threshold-based alerts on incoming readings can fire within a minute. Document your design in DESIGN.md.

code_files.README.md:
# IoT Sensor Ingestion Platform — Design Task

## Task Overview
A connected-devices platform ingests readings from 50,000 field devices that each emit a measurement every 10 seconds. Today, ingestion, the live operations dashboard, and historical trend reports all hit a single relational database, which is causing write latency spikes and stale dashboards during peak hours. You are being asked to redesign the platform so ingestion, real-time queries, and analytical queries no longer contend, and so threshold breaches can be detected and surfaced as alerts within a minute.

## Current System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Devices[50K Field Devices] --> LB[HTTPS Load Balancer]
    LB --> Ingest[FastAPI Ingestion Service]
    Dashboard[Ops Dashboard UI] --> Query[FastAPI Dashboard Service]
    Ingest --> DB[(Primary Relational DB)]
    Query --> DB
    Ops[On-call Engineer] --> Query
```

- 50K devices push readings every 10 seconds over HTTPS
- One FastAPI ingestion service runs on 4 workers and writes each reading directly to the primary database
- One FastAPI dashboard service serves both the live operations UI and ad-hoc historical queries
- Both services share a single primary database with a connection pool of 50 connections per service
- There is no message broker, no caching layer, and no separation between hot and historical data
- Threshold alerts are not currently implemented — the operations team eyeballs the dashboard

## Current Data Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Field Device
    participant LB as Load Balancer
    participant Ing as Ingestion Service
    participant DB as Relational DB
    participant Q as Dashboard Service
    participant UI as Ops Dashboard UI

    Dev->>LB: POST /v1/readings
    LB->>Ing: Forward request
    Ing->>DB: INSERT reading row
    DB-->>Ing: Insert ack
    Ing-->>LB: 202 Accepted
    LB-->>Dev: 202 Accepted

    UI->>Q: GET /dashboard/latest
    Q->>DB: SELECT recent readings
    DB-->>Q: Recent rows
    Q-->>UI: Latest readings JSON
```

- Every device POST results in one synchronous INSERT against the primary database
- The dashboard UI polls the dashboard service every few seconds for the latest readings and aggregates
- Historical trend reports run against the same primary database during business hours
- There is no buffering, batching, or fan-out — every device write hits the database directly

## Design Challenge
- Design a platform that can sustain the device write load without degrading either ingestion latency or query responsiveness
- Define how device readings are accepted, durably captured, and made available to downstream consumers
- Define how the live operations dashboard and historical analytical queries are served without contending with ingestion
- Define how threshold-based alerts on incoming readings are evaluated and surfaced to the operations team
- In scope: the ingestion path, the read/query paths, the alerting path, and storage layering for hot vs cold data
- Out of scope: the device firmware, the UI implementation, identity and access management for human operators
- Deliverable: DESIGN.md — you decide every section, every diagram, every technology choice

## Constraints
- 50,000 active devices, 1 reading per device every 10 seconds, ~5,000 writes/second sustained
- Device reconnection storms after a network outage can deliver up to 10x the steady-state burst for a few minutes
- Live operations dashboard must reflect device readings within 15 seconds of the device transmission
- Historical query workloads commonly span 30 to 90 day windows
- Threshold breaches (e.g. a reading above a configured ceiling) must be detected within 60 seconds
- Data retention: 1 year of online hot storage, then 5 years archived for compliance retrieval
- Current infrastructure: one FastAPI ingestion service on 4 workers, one FastAPI dashboard service, one primary relational database, 50-connection pools per service (described for context only — you decide what stays, what changes, and what is added)
- Budget envelope: up to three production services and the supporting storage / streaming infrastructure of your choice
- Team is comfortable across most modern back-end and data ecosystems; framework familiarity is not a constraint on your choices

## Deliverable
- Write your complete system design in DESIGN.md
- You decide the structure, sections, and level of detail of the document
- Use text descriptions, bullet points, and diagrams (mermaid or ASCII) wherever they make the design clearer
- A strong design document is one another engineer could pick up and use to implement the system without needing to talk to you

## Evaluation Criteria
- Document organization — logical structure, sections that fit the problem, easy to follow
- Completeness — ingestion, real-time query, historical query, alerting, and data lifecycle are all addressed with enough depth to be implementable
- Technology choices with justification — every notable choice (storage, streaming, query engine, alerting) is named and the reasoning is explicit
- Trade-off reasoning — alternatives are considered, the chosen option's downsides are acknowledged, and the bounds of the design are clear
- Clarity — another engineer could read this document and implement from it
- Feasibility — the design works within the stated load, latency, retention, and budget constraints
- Operational readiness — observability, failure modes, back-pressure, and deployment behaviour are addressed, not glossed over

code_files.pyproject.toml:
[project]
name = "iot-platform"
version = "0.4.1"
description = "IoT sensor ingestion and operations platform"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "fastapi==0.109.0",
    "uvicorn[standard]==0.27.0",
    "sqlalchemy[asyncio]==2.0.25",
    "asyncpg==0.29.0",
    "alembic==1.13.1",
    "pydantic==2.5.3",
    "pydantic-settings==2.1.0",
    "httpx==0.26.0",
    "structlog==24.1.0",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest==8.0.0",
    "pytest-asyncio==0.23.3",
    "ruff==0.2.0",
    "mypy==1.8.0",
]

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=68", "wheel"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 100
target-version = "py311"
code_files..env.example:
# Database
DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://iot:iot@localhost:5432/iot_platform
DATABASE_POOL_SIZE=50
DATABASE_MAX_OVERFLOW=10

# App
APP_ENV=local
APP_LOG_LEVEL=INFO

# Ingestion
INGESTION_AUTH_TOKEN=changeme

# Dashboard
DASHBOARD_PAGE_SIZE_DEFAULT=200
DASHBOARD_PAGE_SIZE_MAX=2000
code_files.docker-compose.yml:
version: "3.9"

services:
  db:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: iot
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: iot
      POSTGRES_DB: iot_platform
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - db_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  ingestion:
    build: .
    command: uvicorn app.main:ingestion_app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --workers 4
    env_file:
      - .env
    depends_on:
      - db
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"

  dashboard:
    build: .
    command: uvicorn app.main:dashboard_app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8001 --workers 4
    env_file:
      - .env
    depends_on:
      - db
    ports:
      - "8001:8001"

volumes:
  db_data:

code_files.alembic/env.py:
from logging.config import fileConfig

from alembic import context
from sqlalchemy import engine_from_config, pool

from app.core.config import get_settings
from app.models.base import Base
from app.models import reading  # noqa: F401 — register models

config = context.config
fileConfig(config.config_file_name)

config.set_main_option("sqlalchemy.url", get_settings().database_url_sync)
target_metadata = Base.metadata


def run_migrations_online() -> None:
    connectable = engine_from_config(
        config.get_section(config.config_ini_section),
        prefix="sqlalchemy.",
        poolclass=pool.NullPool,
    )
    with connectable.connect() as connection:
        context.configure(connection=connection, target_metadata=target_metadata)
        with context.begin_transaction():
            context.run_migrations()


run_migrations_online()
code_files.alembic/versions/0001_initial.py:
"""initial schema

Revision ID: 0001_initial
Revises:
Create Date: 2025-08-14 09:12:00
"""
import sqlalchemy as sa
from alembic import op

revision = "0001_initial"
down_revision = None
branch_labels = None
depends_on = None


def upgrade() -> None:
    op.create_table(
        "devices",
        sa.Column("device_id", sa.String(length=64), primary_key=True),
        sa.Column("site_id", sa.String(length=64), nullable=False, index=True),
        sa.Column("model", sa.String(length=64), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("registered_at", sa.DateTime(timezone=True), nullable=False),
    )

    op.create_table(
        "readings",
        sa.Column("id", sa.BigInteger, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True),
        sa.Column("device_id", sa.String(length=64), sa.ForeignKey("devices.device_id"), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("metric", sa.String(length=32), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("value", sa.Float, nullable=False),
        sa.Column("recorded_at", sa.DateTime(timezone=True), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("received_at", sa.DateTime(timezone=True), nullable=False),
    )
    op.create_index("ix_readings_device_recorded", "readings", ["device_id", "recorded_at"])
    op.create_index("ix_readings_recorded", "readings", ["recorded_at"])


def downgrade() -> None:
    op.drop_index("ix_readings_recorded", table_name="readings")
    op.drop_index("ix_readings_device_recorded", table_name="readings")
    op.drop_table("readings")
    op.drop_table("devices")
code_files.src/app/main.py:
from fastapi import FastAPI

from app.api.v1.routes import dashboard as dashboard_routes
from app.api.v1.routes import ingest as ingest_routes
from app.core.config import get_settings
from app.core.logging import configure_logging

_settings = get_settings()
configure_logging(_settings.app_log_level)


def _create_ingestion_app() -> FastAPI:
    app = FastAPI(title="IoT Ingestion", version="0.4.1")
    app.include_router(ingest_routes.router, prefix="/v1")
    return app


def _create_dashboard_app() -> FastAPI:
    app = FastAPI(title="IoT Dashboard", version="0.4.1")
    app.include_router(dashboard_routes.router, prefix="/v1")
    return app


ingestion_app = _create_ingestion_app()
dashboard_app = _create_dashboard_app()
code_files.src/app/core/database.py:
from collections.abc import AsyncIterator

from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession, async_sessionmaker, create_async_engine

from app.core.config import get_settings

_settings = get_settings()

engine = create_async_engine(
    _settings.database_url,
    pool_size=_settings.database_pool_size,
    max_overflow=_settings.database_max_overflow,
    pool_pre_ping=True,
)

SessionLocal = async_sessionmaker(engine, expire_on_commit=False, class_=AsyncSession)


async def get_db_session() -> AsyncIterator[AsyncSession]:
    async with SessionLocal() as session:
        yield session
code_files.src/app/core/logging.py:
import logging

import structlog


def configure_logging(level: str) -> None:
    logging.basicConfig(level=getattr(logging, level.upper(), logging.INFO))
    structlog.configure(
        processors=[
            structlog.processors.add_log_level,
            structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt="iso"),
            structlog.processors.JSONRenderer(),
        ],
        wrapper_class=structlog.make_filtering_bound_logger(
            getattr(logging, level.upper(), logging.INFO)
        ),
    )
code_files.src/app/api/v1/routes/ingest.py:
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, Header, HTTPException, status
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

from app.core.config import get_settings
from app.core.database import get_db_session
from app.schemas.reading import ReadingIn
from app.services.ingest_service import IngestService

router = APIRouter(tags=["ingest"])
_settings = get_settings()


def _authorize(token: str | None) -> None:
    if token != f"Bearer {_settings.ingestion_auth_token}":
        raise HTTPException(status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED, detail="invalid token")


@router.post("/readings", status_code=status.HTTP_202_ACCEPTED)
async def post_reading(
    payload: ReadingIn,
    authorization: str | None = Header(default=None),
    session: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db_session),
) -> dict[str, str]:
    _authorize(authorization)
    service = IngestService(session)
    await service.record(payload)
    return {"status": "accepted"}


@router.post("/readings/batch", status_code=status.HTTP_202_ACCEPTED)
async def post_reading_batch(
    payload: list[ReadingIn],
    authorization: str | None = Header(default=None),
    session: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db_session),
) -> dict[str, int]:
    _authorize(authorization)
    service = IngestService(session)
    await service.record_many(payload)
    return {"accepted": len(payload)}
code_files.src/app/api/v1/routes/dashboard.py:
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, Query
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

from app.core.config import get_settings
from app.core.database import get_db_session
from app.schemas.reading import ReadingOut, WindowAggregate
from app.services.query_service import QueryService

router = APIRouter(tags=["dashboard"])
_settings = get_settings()


@router.get("/readings/latest", response_model=list[ReadingOut])
async def get_latest(
    site_id: str | None = Query(default=None),
    limit: int = Query(default=_settings.dashboard_page_size_default),
    session: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db_session),
) -> list[ReadingOut]:
    capped = min(limit, _settings.dashboard_page_size_max)
    service = QueryService(session)
    return await service.latest_readings(site_id=site_id, limit=capped)


@router.get("/readings/window", response_model=list[WindowAggregate])
async def get_window(
    metric: str,
    minutes: int = Query(default=60, ge=1, le=60 * 24 * 90),
    session: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db_session),
) -> list[WindowAggregate]:
    service = QueryService(session)
    return await service.window_aggregate(metric=metric, minutes=minutes)
code_files.src/app/models/reading.py:
from datetime import datetime

from sqlalchemy import DateTime, Float, ForeignKey, String
from sqlalchemy.orm import Mapped, mapped_column, relationship

from app.models.base import Base


class Device(Base):
    __tablename__ = "devices"

    device_id: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64), primary_key=True)
    site_id: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64), index=True)
    model: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64))
    registered_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(DateTime(timezone=True))

    readings: Mapped[list["Reading"]] = relationship(back_populates="device")


class Reading(Base):
    __tablename__ = "readings"

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    device_id: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(ForeignKey("devices.device_id"))
    metric: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(32))
    value: Mapped[float] = mapped_column(Float)
    recorded_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(DateTime(timezone=True))
    received_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(DateTime(timezone=True))

    device: Mapped[Device] = relationship(back_populates="readings")
code_files.src/app/schemas/reading.py:
from datetime import datetime

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class ReadingIn(BaseModel):
    device_id: str = Field(..., max_length=64)
    metric: str = Field(..., max_length=32)
    value: float
    recorded_at: datetime


class ReadingOut(BaseModel):
    device_id: str
    metric: str
    value: float
    recorded_at: datetime
    received_at: datetime


class WindowAggregate(BaseModel):
    bucket_start: datetime
    metric: str
    avg_value: float
    min_value: float
    max_value: float
    sample_count: int
code_files.src/app/services/ingest_service.py:
from datetime import datetime, timezone

from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

from app.models.reading import Reading
from app.repositories.reading_repository import ReadingRepository
from app.schemas.reading import ReadingIn


class IngestService:
    def __init__(self, session: AsyncSession) -> None:
        self._repo = ReadingRepository(session)

    async def record(self, payload: ReadingIn) -> None:
        row = Reading(
            device_id=payload.device_id,
            metric=payload.metric,
            value=payload.value,
            recorded_at=payload.recorded_at,
            received_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc),
        )
        await self._repo.add(row)
        await self._repo.commit()

    async def record_many(self, payloads: list[ReadingIn]) -> None:
        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
        rows = [
            Reading(
                device_id=p.device_id,
                metric=p.metric,
                value=p.value,
                recorded_at=p.recorded_at,
                received_at=now,
            )
            for p in payloads
        ]
        await self._repo.add_many(rows)
        await self._repo.commit()
code_files.src/app/services/query_service.py:
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

from app.repositories.reading_repository import ReadingRepository
from app.schemas.reading import ReadingOut, WindowAggregate


class QueryService:
    def __init__(self, session: AsyncSession) -> None:
        self._repo = ReadingRepository(session)

    async def latest_readings(self, site_id: str | None, limit: int) -> list[ReadingOut]:
        rows = await self._repo.latest(site_id=site_id, limit=limit)
        return [
            ReadingOut(
                device_id=r.device_id,
                metric=r.metric,
                value=r.value,
                recorded_at=r.recorded_at,
                received_at=r.received_at,
            )
            for r in rows
        ]

    async def window_aggregate(self, metric: str, minutes: int) -> list[WindowAggregate]:
        since = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(minutes=minutes)
        buckets = await self._repo.bucketed_stats(metric=metric, since=since)
        return [WindowAggregate(**b) for b in buckets]
from datetime import datetime

from sqlalchemy import func, select
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

from app.models.reading import Device, Reading


class ReadingRepository:
    def __init__(self, session: AsyncSession) -> None:
        self._session = session

    async def add(self, row: Reading) -> None:
        self._session.add(row)

    async def add_many(self, rows: list[Reading]) -> None:
        self._session.add_all(rows)

    async def commit(self) -> None:
        await self._session.commit()

    async def latest(self, site_id: str | None, limit: int) -> list[Reading]:
        stmt = select(Reading).order_by(Reading.recorded_at.desc()).limit(limit)
        if site_id is not None:
            stmt = stmt.join(Device, Device.device_id == Reading.device_id).where(
                Device.site_id == site_id
            )
        result = await self._session.execute(stmt)
        return list(result.scalars().all())

    async def bucketed_stats(self, metric: str, since: datetime) -> list[dict]:
        bucket = func.date_trunc("minute", Reading.recorded_at).label("bucket_start")
        stmt = (
            select(
                bucket,
                Reading.metric,
                func.avg(Reading.value).label("avg_value"),
                func.min(Reading.value).label("min_value"),
                func.max(Reading.value).label("max_value"),
                func.count().label("sample_count"),
            )
            .where(Reading.metric == metric, Reading.recorded_at >= since)
            .group_by(bucket, Reading.metric)
            .order_by(bucket)
        )
        result = await self._session.execute(stmt)
        return [dict(r._mapping) for r in result.all()]

outcomes: A complete written design covering how device readings are accepted, durably captured, served to the operations dashboard, queried historically, and evaluated against alert thresholds — with every notable technology and architectural choice named and the reasoning made explicit.
short_overview:
- A high-throughput ingestion path is failing because every device write hits the same primary database that also serves the live dashboard and historical reports
- You must design a platform that separates ingestion from real-time queries and historical analytics, and that adds a threshold-based alerting path
- You produce DESIGN.md from scratch — no template, no section headers — and you choose every technology, framework, and infrastructure component yourself
- You are evaluated on document organization, completeness, named technology choices with justification, trade-off reasoning, clarity, feasibility, and operational readiness (observability, failure handling, deployment)
pre_requisites:
- Understanding of multi-service back-end architecture and service boundaries
- Experience with asynchronous I/O, event loops, and back-pressure handling
- Familiarity with relational and time-series storage trade-offs
- Knowledge of message brokers, streaming systems, and delivery guarantees
- Familiarity with caching layers and invalidation strategies
- Understanding of horizontal scaling, worker process configuration, and graceful shutdown
- Awareness of observability practices: structured logging, metrics, distributed tracing
- Ability to express architecture and data flow in mermaid or ASCII diagrams
answer:
A strong design separates the ingestion path from the query and analytics paths. The ingestion service should stop writing every individual device reading synchronously to the primary database. A common reference shape: the ingestion service accepts the POST, performs only the cheapest possible validation, and writes the reading to a durable streaming or queue layer (the candidate names their choice — Kafka, Redpanda, NATS JetStream, AWS Kinesis, RabbitMQ streams are all defensible, with delivery-guarantee and ordering trade-offs that should be explicitly discussed). Downstream consumers then process the stream at their own pace. This breaks the synchronous chain between device-write throughput and database-write capacity, which is the root cause of the 5+ second write latency today.

For storage, a strong answer recognises that one relational primary cannot simultaneously serve 5K writes/second of append-only telemetry, a sub-15-second-fresh dashboard, and 30–90 day analytical queries. Candidates typically split storage by access pattern: a hot store optimised for append + recent-time-window reads (TimescaleDB, ClickHouse, InfluxDB, partitioned PostgreSQL with BRIN indexes, or a cloud-native option like BigQuery / Snowflake for the analytical leg), and a separate path for older data tiering down to cheaper archival storage (S3 with Parquet, glacier-class storage). The 1-year hot / 5-year archive constraint should be tied explicitly to a tiering and retention policy.

The dashboard path should be served by a read-optimised projection — pre-aggregated rollups (1-minute and 5-minute buckets) maintained by a stream consumer, with the live dashboard mostly hitting cached / pre-aggregated rows rather than scanning raw readings. A strong answer names the cache (Redis, Memcached, in-process LRU) and the invalidation / TTL strategy. The historical query path can hit the hot store or the analytical store directly depending on the time window.

The alerting path is naturally a stream consumer too: it subscribes to the ingestion stream, evaluates configured thresholds in memory, debounces / deduplicates breaches per device and per metric, and emits alert events that are delivered to operators (webhook, paging, email — candidate's choice with justification). The 60-second SLA is met as long as the stream consumer keeps up with the firehose; candidates should mention back-pressure, consumer-lag monitoring, and what happens during reconnection storms (the streaming layer absorbs the burst; downstream consumers continue at steady pace).

Operational readiness deserves explicit treatment: structured logging with correlation IDs, metrics on ingestion-rate / consumer-lag / cache-hit / DB-pool-saturation, distributed tracing across services, graceful shutdown for the ingestion service (drain in-flight requests before terminating workers), and a deployment strategy that won't drop device writes during rollouts (the streaming layer + idempotent consumers make this much easier than the current direct-write design).

Downsides and trade-offs the candidate should acknowledge: the streaming layer is new operational surface area; eventual consistency between ingestion and the read store means "latest" is bounded by consumer lag rather than instant; analytical-store choice involves cost vs. familiarity; running both a hot store and an analytical store doubles the storage footprint until tiering kicks in. The strongest answers name these honestly rather than presenting the redesign as cost-free.
hints: Consider how decoupling the device-write path from the database-write path via a durable streaming layer would let ingestion, the live dashboard, the historical analytics, and the alerting evaluator all run independently at their own pace.

definitions.back-pressure: A mechanism by which a downstream consumer signals an upstream producer that it cannot keep up, so the producer slows down, buffers, or sheds load rather than overwhelming the system
definitions.CQRS: Command Query Responsibility Segregation — separating the write model (commands) from the read model (queries) so each can be optimised, scaled, and stored independently
definitions.materialized view: A read-optimised, pre-computed projection of underlying data, kept up to date on a defined cadence so that read queries can hit the projection instead of recomputing aggregates from raw rows
definitions.consumer lag: The amount of unprocessed data sitting between the latest message produced to a stream and the latest message a given consumer has acknowledged — a primary health signal for streaming pipelines
definitions.tiered storage: A storage strategy where data lives on progressively cheaper, slower media as it ages — typically hot (recent, frequent access), warm (less frequent), and cold (archival, rare retrieval)
definitions.graceful shutdown: A termination flow in which a service stops accepting new work, finishes or hands off in-flight work, releases external resources cleanly, and only then exits — preventing dropped requests and partial writes during deployments
